text: Year of the World 3049, Year before Christ 955.; 3 Kings xv.
Year of the World 3063, Year before Christ 941.
Years, dating from the fifth; as in the fifteenth year of his reign, Asa offered many victims, which had been taken from the enemy, 2 Paralipomenon 15:10. (Calmet)

God, with respect to the external worship of the true God, (Haydock) though he had some faults, 3 Kings 15:14.<hr>Places, while he tolerated those which were intended for the service of the Lord, (Calmet) though illegally; and he even left the buildings erected by Solomon standing, (4 Kings 23:13.; Tirinus) thinking they might be no longer dangerous. (Salien)<hr>Several other pious kings did the like, for which they deserved some reprehension; as they could not be ignorant, that this was considered as wrong by the more enlightened, Ecclesiasticus 49:5. (Tirinus)<hr>It was the king's duty to destroy the exterior practices of idolatry, while the priests and prophets were to instruct. (Worthington)

Altars. Hebrew, "high places, and the Chamanim," or temples of the sun, Leviticus 26:30. (Calmet)<hr>Protestants, "images." (Haydock)<hr>Asa was encouraged to perfect the work which he had begun, by the exhortation of Azarias, 2 Paralipomenon 15:8. (Calmet)
Time, as yet. (Tirinus)<hr>Admirable lesson for all to prepare for war in time of quiet! (Haydock)

Ethiopian. Some think Zara was a successor of Sesac; and the Rabbins say, that he "restored the spoils which the latter had taken." (Seder. xvi.)<hr>He at least, made the Jews some compensation for that loss. Perhaps he had been present in the expedition of Sesac, and was now animated with the same ambition. (Tirinus)<hr>It would be difficult for a foreign king to traverse Egypt with so large an army, (Calmet) unless he had conquered or intimidated the king of the country. (Haydock)<hr>Hence some believe that the Ethiopia here in question, was a part of Arabia. (Calmet) (Torniel.)<hr>But it is more probable that it lay south of Egypt, as the troops of Zara are joined with the Lybians, 2 Paralipomenon 16:8. (Salien, the year of the world 3094.) (Tirinus)<hr>A king of Arabia would hardly have had so many subjects. (Calmet)<hr>Three. Some would read thirteen, as Anani takes notice of the great number of chariots, 2 Paralipomenon 16:8. (Junius) (Malvenda) (Calmet)<hr>But surely the number here specified is sufficient. The minds of men are very different. In the former chapter we find them complaining of the too great numbers! (Haydock)

Meet him, fearing lest he should lay waste his dominions, as Sesac had done. (Menochius)<hr>Sephata, or Sephala, a great valley to the west of Maresa, (Calmet) which is a city of Juda. (Menochius)<hr>Septuagint have, "in the vale north of Maresa." (Haydock)

Help. Hebrew, "to help; whether with many, or with those who have no power." The same sentiment was expressed by Jonathas, 1 Kings 14:6. Those who have God with them are secure of victory. (Calmet)<hr>If God be with us, who is against us?
Terrified. Hebrew and Septuagint, "struck," as he had done Jeroboam, 2 Paralipomenon 13:15. (Haydock)

Gerara was on the southern borders, twenty-three miles from Maresa. (Eusebius) (Calmet)<hr>The Ethiopians were pursued perhaps for many days. (Tirinus)<hr>Destruction. Protestants, "that they could not recover themselves." Hebrew, "there was no quarter given;" or, "so that none were left alive." (Calmet)<hr>If this were the case, the number of the slain far exceeded that the Jeroboam's army, 2 Paralipomenon 13. (Haydock)

Gerara. The adjacent territory is now uncultivated, Josue 13:3.<hr>Great. Hebrew, "a fear of the Lord;" (ver. 12) an extraordinary (Calmet) panic. (Haydock)<hr>The Philistines had assisted the enemy, and therefore their cities were invaded; (Menochius) or these belonged to Zara. (Calmet)

Destroyed. Hebrew, "smote also the tents of cattle." Septuagint, "they cut in pieces the tents of cattle, the Amazons;" or, according to the Vatican copy, (Haydock) "the Alimazonians," as they seem to have read aeli manim, which may be well rendered, "the tents of the Mineans," a people of Arabia, 2 Paralipomenon 20:1., and 26:7., and Judges 10:11. (Calmet)<hr>If these nations came to punish Asa, for banishing their foreign worship, (ver. 12) as the Sieur de Folard thinks the sacred penman insinuates, (ver. 5) we need not wonder that they met with such ill success, and that not one of them was spared, as God avenged his own cause, ver. 12, 13. (Haydock)<hr>We read of no cavalry here, though it is mentioned, 2 Paralipomenon 16:8. It seems not to have been considerable, as the people of Asia, the Greeks and Romans, trusted more to their infantry; and, in effect, no species of warfare is more costly to a state, nor less useful, than a multitude of horses, which were introduced in barbarous times. (De Folard)
